1: Psalm 35 is an individual lament in three clearly marked sections (Psalms 35:1–10, 11–18, 19–28), each ending with a vow to praise God in the assembly. The opening imperative "Contend (rîbāh), O LORD, against those who contend with me" is a lawsuit term: rîb is the legal dispute, the pleading of a case in court. The psalmist summons YHWH as his legal advocate — the one who will argue his case before the divine court. This forensic framing (also Psalms 43:1; Isaiah 49:25; Jeremiah 2:9; Psalms 51:36; Micah 7:9) reflects the ancient Israelite institution of the divine judge as the ultimate court of appeal for those who cannot obtain justice in human courts.
2: "Take up your shield (māgēn) and armor (ṣinnāh) and come to my aid" — the divine warrior motif, in which YHWH is imagined as a fully armed soldier fighting for his people. Two types of shield are mentioned: the māgēn (small round shield carried on the arm) and the ṣinnāh (the large full-body shield). The armament request is hyperbolic — the psalmist calls on the entire arsenal of the divine warrior. This divine warrior imagery is central to the Exodus tradition (Exodus 14:14; Psalms 15:3), the prophets (Isaiah 42:13; Psalms 59:16–17; Zephaniah 3:17), and appears in the armor of God metaphor of Ephesians 6:10–17.
3: "Brandish the spear (ḥănît) and block the path" — the specific weapons named here are those of the professional warrior class: the ḥănît (long battle-spear) was the characteristic weapon of the Philistine giant (1 Samuel 17:7; 2 Samuel 21:19). The divine warrior who carries such weapons surpasses even the feared Philistine heavy infantry. "Say to my soul, 'I am your deliverance (yĕšûʿātĕkā)!'" — YHWH speaking into the nepeš (soul, inner self) is the personal dimension of divine assurance: not just external military action but an inner word of peace. The term yĕšûʿāh (salvation, deliverance) is the root of the name Yeshua/Jesus.
4: "Let those who seek my life be put to shame (yēbōšû) and humiliated" — the shame petition is a recurring feature of lament psalms (Psalms 25:3; Psalms 31:17; Psalms 40:14; Psalms 69:6; Psalms 70:2; Psalms 71:24). In ANE honor-shame culture, shame before the community was the severest social consequence — a reversal of social standing. The petition is not merely personal vindication but the public restoration of honor that has been taken away by the enemy's slanders and attacks. "Those who plot evil against me be turned back in confusion (wĕyissōgû ʾāḥôr wĕyikālamû)" — the double movement of retreat and disgrace.
5: "Let them be like chaff (môṣ) before the wind" — the threshing floor analogy. In ancient Israelite agriculture, grain was threshed by trampling (oxen or teams walking over the stalks) and then winnowed by tossing into the air so the wind could carry away the chaff while the grain fell. The chaff that blew away was worthless, insubstantial, easily dispersed. This imagery appears in Psalms 1:4 for the wicked in contrast to the righteous person who is like a firmly planted tree. "The angel of the LORD driving them off" — the same divine warrior-messenger who protected the righteous in Psalms 34:7 here pursues the wicked.
6: "Make their path dark and treacherous (ḥălaqlaqqôt), with the angel of the LORD pursuing them" — ḥălaqlaqqôt (slippery, treacherous places) from ḥālaq (smooth, slippery). In hilly terrain, smooth rock faces and loose scree were genuinely dangerous; the metaphor of a slippery path for those being pursued invokes the genuine terror of flight through hostile terrain (cf. Jeremiah 23:12; Psalms 73:18). The pursuing angel here mirrors the pursuing angel of Numbers 22:22–27, who blocked Balaam's path. The divine pursuer makes escape impossible.
7: "Without reason (ḥinnām) they concealed their net to trap me; without reason they dug a pit for my life" — ḥinnām (without cause, gratuitously, for nothing) is the same word used in Job 1:9 when the satan asks if Job fears God "for nothing" (ḥinnām), and in Job 2:3 where God says the satan moved against Job "without cause." The word marks the moral bankruptcy of the attack: there is no legitimate grievance, no provocation, no justification. Hunting traps (nets, pits) were the standard OT metaphor for hidden schemes and ambushes (Psalms 9:15; Psalms 31:4; Psalms 57:6; Psalms 140:5; Psalms 141:9–10; Jeremiah 18:22).
8: "May ruin overtake him when he least expects it" — the boomerang principle: the trap designed for the righteous catches the trapper instead. This retributive pattern runs through the Psalter and wisdom literature (Proverbs 26:27; Ecclesiastes 10:8; Sir Psalms 27:26–27). "The net he hid catch him; may he fall into it to his destruction (bĕšôʾāh)" — šôʾāh is the same root as the Hebrew word for the Holocaust (destruction, catastrophic ruin). The poetic justice is complete: the pit dug for another becomes one's own grave.
9: "Then my soul will rejoice in the LORD, exulting in his deliverance" — the vow-of-praise formula that ends the first section. The future joy anticipated in prayer is a distinctive feature of the lament-to-praise movement in the Psalter: the psalmist commits in advance to praise the God who has not yet acted, expressing confidence that deliverance is coming. This "anticipatory praise" (sometimes called the "psalm of trust" element within laments) is the most distinctive theological contribution of the lament genre: praise uttered in the darkness before the dawn.
10: "Every bone in my body will say, 'LORD, who is like you?'" — the totality of the person (every bone = the whole physical self) engaged in praise. "Who rescues the poor (ʿānî) from those who overpower them" — the divine champion of the weak is one of the central themes of the Psalter and the prophets. The rhetorical question mî kāmôkā (who is like you?) is the incomparability formula, the most emphatic assertion of monotheistic uniqueness. It appears in the Song of the Sea (Exodus 15:11), in the prophets (Isaiah 40:18; Psalms 46:5; Micah 7:18), and throughout the Psalter as the climactic proclamation of divine uniqueness.
11: "Malicious witnesses (ʿēdê ḥāmās) rise against me; they charge me with things I know nothing of" — the legal setting is explicit. ʿēd ḥāmās (witness of violence/malice) is the false witness condemned in the Decalogue (Exodus 20:16: "do not give false testimony") and in the Deuteronomic law of witnesses (Deuteronomy 19:15–21). In the legal system of ancient Israel, testimony by two or three witnesses was required for conviction; false accusers who bore false witness received the penalty that would have been inflicted on the accused (Deuteronomy 19:19). The psalmist faces the worst social-legal threat of the ancient world: coordinated perjury before an Israelite court.
12: "They repay me evil for good, leaving my soul (napšî) desolate (šĕkôl)" — the inversion of reciprocity. In ANE honor culture, reciprocal gift exchange and loyalty were the social glue of community life. To repay evil for good was the most profound violation of social covenant (cf. Proverbs 17:13; Genesis 44:4; 1 Samuel 25:21; John 10:32). Šĕkôl (desolate, bereaved, like a parent whose child has died) — the root of šĕkôl (bereavement after the death of children); to be left šĕkôl is to experience the most devastating social loss known in antiquity, the cutting off of one's line.
13: "But when they were sick, I wore sackcloth (śāq) and humbled myself with fasting" — sackcloth was a coarse garment of dark goat-hair worn as the standard dress of mourning, fasting, and penitence throughout the ancient Near East (1 Kings 21:27; 2 Kings 6:30; Nehemiah 9:1; Isaiah 58:5; Jonah 3:5–8). Donning sackcloth for someone else's illness was the ultimate expression of solidarity — treating their pain as your own grief. Fasting accompanied sackcloth as the bodily practice of prayer, intensifying the petition (Psalms 69:10; Psalms 109:24; Ezra 8:23; Esther 4:3).
14: "I acted like one grieving for a dear friend (ʾōhēb) or brother; I bowed down in mourning as one who weeps for his mother" — the depth of social solidarity expressed here is remarkable: the psalmist mourned for his enemies as if they were his closest kin — friend, brother, mother. ANE mourning customs included wearing sackcloth, tearing garments, putting dust or ashes on the head, refusing to eat, and verbal lamentation (Genesis 37:34; 2 Samuel 1:11–12; Psalms 3:31–32; Psalms 13:19). To perform all these customs for an enemy who has now turned on you is the deepest form of social betrayal: the person you wept for now brings false charges against you.
15: "But when I stumbled, they rejoiced and gathered together; attackers assembled against me" — Schadenfreude (joy at another's misfortune) was a recognized moral vice in both Israel and the ancient Near East. Ezekiel 25:3, 6 and Lamentations 2:15–16 record the enemies of Israel rejoicing over Jerusalem's fall with the same šāmaḥ (rejoice) verb. The gathering of accusers and attackers when the psalmist stumbled suggests a social situation in which a change of fortune (illness, legal setback, military defeat) was treated as evidence of guilt — the friends who were mourned now become accusers.
16: "Like godless mockers at a banquet (ḥanpê-laʿăgê māʿôg), they gnashed their teeth at me" — a notoriously difficult Hebrew phrase. Ḥanpê (godless, profane) + laʿăgê (mockers) + māʿôg (cake, pastry; or a place name). The most defensible reading: "like the profane mockers at a feast [who mock the absent]" — the banquet setting where mockery and slander of the absent (table gossip, symposium wit at others' expense) was a recognized social practice. "Gnashing teeth" (ḥāraq šinnayim) is the physical expression of aggressive rage or contempt (Psalms 37:12; Psalms 112:10; Lamentations 2:16; Acts 7:54).
17: "How long, O Lord (ʾădōnāy), will you keep watching (tirʾeh)?" — the "how long?" question is the characteristic temporal challenge of the lament psalm (Psalms 6:3; Psalms 13:1–2; Psalms 74:10; Psalms 80:4; Psalms 90:13; Psalms 94:3; Habakkuk 1:2; Psalms 2:6). Divine watching without acting is experienced as cruel indifference — the judge who sees the crime and does nothing. "Rescue my precious self (napšî) from these lions" — the lion metaphor returns (cf. Psalms 35:10 above; Psalms 22:12–13, 21). The yĕḥîdāh (my precious one, my only one) is a particularly intimate self-designation — one's unique, irreplaceable life.
18: "I will give you thanks in the great assembly (qāhāl rāb); among the vast multitude (ʿam ʿāṣûm) I will praise you" — the second vow of praise closes the second section. The public assembly is a key setting for thanksgiving in the Psalter. The peace offering feast (cf. Psalms 34:8 above) was performed in the temple courts and included a public proclamation of the deliverance received (cf. Psalms 22:22–25; Psalms 40:9–10; Psalms 66:16–19; Psalms 116:14–19). To vow public praise is to commit to the social performance of gratitude — the testimony that invites the community into the psalmist's experience of divine faithfulness.
19: "Do not let my enemies gloat over me without cause (ḥinnām); do not let those who hate me without reason (šĕqer) exchange knowing glances" — ḥinnām appears again (cf. Psalms 35:7): the gratuitous, unprovoked nature of the hostility is emphasized. "Exchange knowing glances (yiqrĕṣû-ʿāyin)" — the wink or meaningful glance exchanged between conspirators was the non-verbal communication of shared deception in ancient social settings (Proverbs 6:13; Psalms 10:10). It was the signal of those who coordinate slander without using words that could be overheard.
20: "They do not pursue peace (šālôm); instead they invent slanders (mirmôt dĕbārê) against those who live quietly in the land" — the "quiet in the land" (rĕgîʿê ʾāreṣ) is an unusual phrase sometimes taken as a technical term for the peaceful peasant class, those living without political prominence and without causing harm. The attack on those who "live quietly" — who seek no conflict — makes the hostility even more gratuitous. This is not a conflict between powers; it is the persecution of the unassuming by the politically connected. Second Temple Jewish interpretation often read the "quiet in the land" as the pious remnant (ʿănāwîm) persecuted by the powerful.
21: "They open their mouths wide against me and say, 'Aha! Aha! (heʾāḥ heʾāḥ) We have seen it with our own eyes!'" — the exclamation heʾāḥ is a gloating cry of triumph at another's downfall, used for mock-triumph over an enemy (Psalms 40:15; Psalms 70:3; Ezekiel 25:3; Psalms 26:2; Psalms 36:2). The claim "we have seen it with our own eyes" is a legal formula — eyewitness testimony was the strongest form of evidence in ancient courts. The adversaries are manufacturing false eyewitness accounts, the severest form of legal perjury.
22: "You have seen this (rāʾîtāh), LORD; do not stay silent (al-teḥĕraš)" — the psalmist turns the courtroom language against the accusers: YHWH has seen what they claim to have witnessed; divine testimony trumps human perjury. "Do not stay silent" — divine silence in the face of injustice is experienced as complicity (cf. Habakkuk 1:2, 13; Psalms 83:1). The petition for YHWH to speak is simultaneously a petition for action: when God speaks, things happen.
23: "Arise (hāʿîrāh) and wake yourself (hāqîṣāh) to my defense" — the "rise up" imperative battle-cry (cf. Numbers 10:35; Psalms 3:7; Psalms 7:6; Psalms 9:19; Psalms 10:12) is here doubled with "wake yourself," creating an image of the sleeping deity who must be roused. This anthropomorphism reaches its most dramatic expression in Psalms 44:23 ("Awake, O Lord! Why do you sleep?") and its gentle negation in Psalms 121:4 ("He who guards Israel will neither slumber nor sleep"). The tension between the sleeping/silent God and the responsive God is honestly maintained in the Psalter without easy resolution.
24: "Vindicate me, LORD my God, according to your righteousness (ṣidqĕkā)" — the appeal to divine righteousness as the basis of vindication invokes the covenant character of YHWH. Ṣādaqāh (righteousness) in the legal sphere means the right order of things — what is due to each party in a dispute. God's righteousness is not a demanding standard the psalmist must meet but the character that drives God to restore right order when it has been violated. The same appeal appears in Psalms 31:1; Psalms 71:2; Psalms 143:1, 11.
25: "Do not let them say in their hearts, 'Aha, just what we wanted!'" — the language of desire satisfied (heʾāḥ napšênû, literally "our soul says aha!") echoes the gloating of Psalms 35:21. The petition reaches into the adversaries' inner life — not merely their outward triumph but the inner satisfaction of appetite achieved. "We have devoured him (bĕlaʿănûhû)" — the swallowing image (cf. Lamentations 2:16; Psalms 124:3) presents the enemy as predator consuming prey, the apex-predator imagery of the psalms of persecution.
26: "Let all who rejoice at my misfortune be put to shame and humiliated together" — the shame-reversal petition: those who enjoyed the psalmist's shame will experience shame themselves. "Clothed with shame and disgrace (ʿōṭû-bōšet ûkĕlimmāh)" — shame is a garment that visibly marks the one who wears it in ancient honor-shame culture. The image of being "clothed" in shame or glory occurs throughout the Psalter and the prophets (Psalms 93:1 — YHWH clothed in majesty; Psalms 109:18 — the wicked clothed in curses; Isaiah 61:3 — the garment of praise for the spirit of heaviness).
27: "Let those who delight in my vindication (ṣidqî) shout for joy and say, 'Great is the LORD, who takes pleasure in the welfare (šālôm) of his servant!'" — the communal dimension of individual vindication: the psalmist's restoration is not only a personal event but a liturgical occasion for all who have invested hope in YHWH's justice. "Those who delight in my vindication" are the solidarity community — the pious who have prayed for the falsely accused. YHWH's "pleasure in the welfare of his servant" (ḥāpēṣ šĕlôm ʿabdô) grounds the petition in divine character: the God who takes pleasure in human flourishing is the basis for expecting deliverance.
28: "Then my tongue will proclaim your righteousness and your praise all day long" — the third and final vow of praise closes the psalm. The temporal marker "all day long" (kol-hayyôm) indicates the continuous, unending character of the praise: not a single act of thanksgiving but a lifetime orientation. The psalm began with a forensic challenge (contend for me, Psalms 35:1) and ends with a public declaration (my tongue will proclaim) — the movement from crisis to testimony, from silence to speech, from fear to praise, is the structural arc of the entire Psalter's lament genre.
